<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966132 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05191: Decomposing model

- page_id: `243966132`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966132/Decomposing+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:49:53.359+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with projects > Project partitioning
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

305246055

305246057

305246056

Model decomposition has a package level granularity. Smaller elements cannot be split into separate projects. Basically, each project package could be partitioned into separate projects; however, this is excessive.

The decision on how to split a model into parts should be made carefully. You should isolate model parts that form logically complete pieces of a structure (e.g., a subsystem, code library, or profile) and are not very dependent on each other.

When there are many one-way dependencies to a model part (e.g., parts **A**, **B**, **C** depend on a part **D**, but part **D** does not depend on any of the parts **A**, **B**, **C**), this part is a good candidate for placement into a separate project.

When one big project is used to store all modeling information (e.g., use case models, high level architectural models, detailed implementation level class, sequence, state, and other information), it may be useful to partition the models according to the modeling domains (i.e., use cases in one used project, architectural models in another, implementation level models in yet another). This allows unloading unnecessary used projects while working on one part (and improving the performance), while retaining the relationships between domains and loading used projects on demand.

Avoid decomposing a model into parts which have circular dependencies. That is, A ↔ B or A → B → C → A situations.

Usually programmers are very adept at splitting large code bases into libraries. The very same criteria should be applied for splitting large models into separate projects.

The project decomposition functionality allows two important options:

- Working without all used projects loaded.
- Using the project in the read-write accessibility mode.

Used projects are often exploited for storing profiles. However, a used project is not a profile, and it is important not to mix them. Any model part can be stored in a separate project.

305246054

**Related pages**

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ca51f05-cf6e-45be-8808-8cce1943a178"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305246055</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="854fef02-859d-4f06-8890-d511224a5d11"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305246057</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="02ee303f-cab6-4a39-b8db-af8a9bacc87d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305246056</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Model decomposition has a package level granularity. Smaller elements cannot be split into separate projects. Basically, each project package could be partitioned into separate projects; however, this is excessive.</p><p>The decision on how to split a model into parts should be made carefully. You should isolate model parts that form logically complete pieces of a structure (e.g., a subsystem, code library, or profile) and are not very dependent on each other.</p><p>When there are many one-way dependencies to a model part (e.g., parts <strong>A</strong>, <strong>B</strong>, <strong>C</strong> depend on a part <strong>D</strong>, but part <strong>D</strong> does not depend on any of the parts <strong>A</strong>, <strong>B</strong>, <strong>C</strong>), this part is a good candidate for placement into a separate project.</p><p>When one big project is used to store all modeling information (e.g., use case models, high level architectural models, detailed implementation level class, sequence, state, and other information), it may be useful to partition the models according to the modeling domains (i.e., use cases in one used project, architectural models in another, implementation level models in yet another). This allows unloading unnecessary used projects while working on one part (and improving the performance), while retaining the relationships between domains and loading used projects on demand.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b77ac731-7a31-4bfe-9436-2348fb7622de"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Avoid decomposing a model into parts which have circular dependencies. That is, A ↔ B or A → B <span>→</span> C <span>→</span> A situations.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Usually programmers are very adept at splitting large code bases into libraries. The very same criteria should be applied for splitting large models into separate projects.</p><p>The project decomposition functionality allows two important options:</p><ul><li>Working without all used projects loaded.</li><li>Using the project in the read-write accessibility mode.</li></ul><p>Used projects are often exploited for storing profiles. However, a used project is not a profile, and it is important not to mix them. Any model part can be stored in a separate project.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae3e38bf-16af-4488-bb2f-d7bc238ddeeb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305246054</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="26a99b5b-7c20-44f1-8461-6c24f9ac88eb" /><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968199 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05192: Default property values

- page_id: `243968199`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968199/Default+property+values
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-09T15:31:21.472+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The modeling tool allows you to define the initial (default) properties for elements.

The Default element properties can be defined for:

- The whole project
- The specific diagram

To set the default properties for the whole project

1. From the Options menu, select Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Expand Default model properties . Select the desired elements and change their property values.

After creating a new element it will have new property values. Values for previously created elements will not be changed.

To reset element properties to the default value

- In he Project Options dialog, Default model properties group, click the Reset to Defaults button.

Exception: interface attribute default visibility will always be #public, no matter what your settings are.

To set default properties for the specific diagram

1. From the Diagrams menu, select Customize . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing diagrams' space='UPDG'] dialog opens.
2. Select the diagram type you want to customize.
3. Click the Edit button. The Customize Diagram Wizard opens.
4. In the fourth step Specify toolbar buttons , click the Add button and select New Button . The Edit Button dialog opens.
5. In the **General** > **Model Element Type** drop-down list, select the element, which default properties you want to customize.
6. In the **Edit Button** dialog, open the **Element Properties** tab.
7. Select the **Specify own values** radio button. Now change the default property values of the chosen element.

Create a new element from the customized diagram toolbar and the element default properties will be the same as you had defined.

##### Sharing the default property values

You can share the default properties with other users for their new project. After changing the property values in the **Project Options** dialog, create a project template.

To create a template

1. From the File menu select Export > Template
2. Enter the template name and description.
3. Click OK . A template will be created in <moleding tool installation directory>\ templates .

To import a template

1. From the File menu, select New Project . The New Project dialog opens.
2. From the Other domain, select the Project from Template icon.
3. In the Select template tree, choose your template. The project options are imported to the project together with the template.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561422 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05193: Default Rollup Pattern Blocks

- page_id: `249561422`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561422/Default+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:40:10.501+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks > Rollup Pattern Wizard
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1348391193

1348391196

1348391194

You can apply the default or[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new Rollup Pattern Block' space=''] by using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rollup Pattern Wizard' space='']**Rollup Pattern** **Wizard**. If you want to apply total cost, mass, or powercalculation for your model, you can use the default rollup patterns. The default rollup pattern Blocks are as follows:

- CostRollupPattern.
- MassRollupPattern.
- PowerRollupPattern.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The total cost, mass, or power rollup pattern Blocks defined in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.

The default rollup patterns are created to calculate the total cost, mass, and power of the model elements. Each pattern Block contains a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space=''] that defines the calculation needs, and allows you to define any kind of rollup pattern. The patterns are created in association with a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='MED'] that defines the logic of the constraints. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space='MED'] type is a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='MED']. The following figure demonstrates the Parametric Diagram of Cost Rollup Pattern.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The SysML Parametric Diagram of Cost Rollup Pattern.

All default rollup patterns are stored in each project under the auxiliary resources.The procedure below describes how to find them in your project.

To find default rollup pattern Blocks in a project

1. In the Containment tree, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''], and select the **Show Auxiliary Resources** option.
2. Select package *MD Customization for SysML* > *rollup patterns*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The default rollup pattern Blocks are stored in the model under the auxiliary resources.

1348391192

**Related pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rollup Pattern Wizard' space='']

**Webinar**

- Total Mass, Cost, and Power Rollups

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figures of this page is the **Laptop Cost Analysis** that comes with [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation' space='MMA']. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='LaptopCostAnalysis.mdzip'].*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\simulation\LaptopCostAnalysis.mdzip

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968384 space=MT version=7 -->
## PAGE 05194: Default settings of hyperlinks to model elements

- page_id: `243968384`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968384/Default+settings+of+hyperlinks+to+model+elements
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-09-11T19:49:11.118+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Manage Navigation > Defining hyperlinks > Hyperlinks in texts
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When adding hyperlinks in text to refer to model elements, the values of **Text to display box**is **Name** and **Update Mode**is **Automatically updated** are selected by default. You can change the default settings in the **Project Options** dialog.

To change the default settings of a hyperlink to model element

1. From the main menu, select Options > Project .
2. In the **Project Options** dialog , expand the General property group in the tree.
3. Select Element References .
4. Select the option for the value you want to change:
  - Select one of the Default Element Hyperlink Display Mode option values: - **Name**(Default). Displays the element name from its Specification window. 
- **Representation text.** Displays the full element title visible in the Containment tree. 
- **Icon+Representation text*.*** Displays the element icon together with the full title from the Containment tree. 
- **Custom text**. Allows you to type your own text in the text box; however, then you cannot specify the **Update mode** option.
  - Select one of the Default Element Hyperlink Update Mode option values: - Automatically updated (Default) . Updates hyperlink text or the referenced element name automatically. - Automatically checked . Enables active validation rules which check if the hyperlink and the referenced element are coherent. - Manually checked. Allows you to run validation manually to check if the hyperlink and the referenced element are coherent. - Do not update . Does not update if the hyperlink text and the referenced element name become inconsistent. Validation will not find any inconsistencies.
5. Click OK .

The default settings of hyperlinks to elements are changed.

**Related pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks' space='']

- Managing hyperlinks
- Linking model elements to OSLC resources

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Displays the element name from its Specification window.<br />- <strong>Representation text.</strong> Displays the full element title visible in the Containment tree. <br />- <strong>Icon+Representation text<em>.</em></strong> Displays the element icon together with the full title from the Containment tree.<br />- <strong>Custom text</strong>. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562366 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05195: Default SysML v1 values and instance specifications

- page_id: `249562366`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562366/Default+SysML+v1+values+and+instance+specifications
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:36:16.203+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Hydraulics sample
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1. Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the fluidReservoir1, fluidReservoir2 and pipe part properties in the ConnectedTanks internal block diagram. Instance specifications t1 , t2 and p are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties fluidReservoir1 , fluidReservoir2 , pipe respectively. These specifications assign an initial values to the value properties that are constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints.
2. The Tank block is a classifier of t1 and t2 instance specifications with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:
  1. **tanksufaceArea**: This slot determines the surface area of the tank, and is required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes. The unit is m2.
  2. **gravity**: This slot is the gravity constant assumed to be 9.8 m/s2. It is is also required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes.
  3. **fluidDensity**: This is a slot of the fluid in the tank itself that is coupled with and assigned to the tank. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of fluid pressure in the tank. The unit for this property is kg/m3.
  4. fluidLevel : This slot determines the height of the fluid level in each tank at the start of the simulation but will change as the simulation runs and the fluid level height changes with the flow of fluid from one tank to the other. Their units are m.
3. The Pipe block is a classifier of p instance specification with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:
  1. pipeLength : This is a slot of the pipe that determines the resistance of the pipe to the flow of fluid through the pipe. The longer the pipe, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.
  2. radius : Assuming the pipe has a circular cross-section, the radius is required to determine the cross-sectional area of the pipe. This is in turn required to determine the resistance of the pipe to the flow fluid through the pipe. The smaller the radius, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.
  3. dynamicViscosity : This is a slot of the fluid in the pipe itself that is coupled with and assigned to the pipe. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of resistance to fluid flow in the pipe. The unit for this property is kg/(s*m).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ol><li>Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the fluidReservoir1, fluidReservoir2 and <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">pipe</em> part properties in the<em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> ConnectedTanks</em> internal block diagram. Instance specifications <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">t1</em>, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">t2</em> and<em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> p</em> are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">fluidReservoir1</em>, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">fluidReservoir2</em>, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">pipe</em> respectively. These specifications assign an initial values to the value properties that are constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints.</li><li>The <em>Tank</em> block is a classifier of t1 and t2 instance specifications with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:<ol><li><p><strong>tanksufaceArea</strong>: This slot determines the surface area of the tank, and is required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes. The unit is m2.</p></li><li><p><strong>gravity</strong>: This slot is the gravity constant assumed to be 9.8 m/s2. It is is also required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes.</p></li><li><p><strong>fluidDensity</strong>: This is a slot of the fluid in the tank itself that is coupled with and assigned to the tank. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of fluid pressure in the tank. The unit for this property is kg/m3.</p></li><li><strong>fluidLevel</strong>: This slot determines the height of the fluid level in each tank at the start of the simulation but will change as the simulation runs and the fluid level height changes with the flow of fluid from one tank to the other. Their units are m.</li></ol></li><li>The <em>Pipe</em> block is a classifier of <em>p</em> instance specification with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:<ol><li><strong>pipeLength</strong>: This is a slot of the pipe that determines the resistance of the pipe to the flow of fluid through the pipe. The longer the pipe, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.</li><li><strong>radius</strong>: Assuming the pipe has a circular cross-section, the radius is required to determine the cross-sectional area of the pipe. This is in turn required to determine the resistance of the pipe to the flow fluid through the pipe. The smaller the radius, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.</li><li><strong>dynamicViscosity</strong>: This is a slot of the fluid in the pipe itself that is coupled with and assigned to the pipe. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of resistance to fluid flow in the pipe. The unit for this property is kg/(s*m).</li></ol></li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="containtment_tree_hydraulics_model.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562423 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05196: Default SysML v1 values and specifications of the instance

- page_id: `249562423`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562423/Default+SysML+v1+values+and+specifications+of+the+instance
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:35:06.406+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Signal processor sample
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1. The *SignalSource*, *Amplifier,**HighPassFilter*, and*LowPassFilter* blocks are classifiers of *ss*, *ampfr*, *hpfvar* and *lpfvar* instance specifications. Each have a slot with numeric value defined that stays constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints. The value of slot *alfa* in each of the *hpfvar* and *lpfvar*instance specifications are required to determine the cut-off frequency for the signal filters. The value of slot *g*in *ampfr* instance specification**is required to determine the gain factor of the *Amplifier* block. The value of slot *amp* in the ss instance specification determines the amplitude of the generated signal.
2. Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the two filter part properties and amplifier part property (parts *hPF*, *lPF* and*a*) in the *SignalProcessor* internal block diagram. Instance specifications *hpfvar*, *lpfvar*and*ampfr*are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties *hPF*, *lPF* and*a,* respectively. These specifications assign an initial value for the unit-less variable*x* in each part that is required for filtering the signal in the signal processor at the start of the simulation. But as the simulation runs, these values do change. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ol><li><p>The <em>SignalSource</em>, <em>Amplifier, </em><em>HighPassFilter</em>, and<em> LowPassFilter</em> blocks are classifiers of <em>ss</em>, <em>ampfr</em>, <em>hpfvar</em> and <em>lpfvar</em> instance specifications. Each have a slot with numeric value defined that stays constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints. The value of slot <em>alfa</em> in each of the <em>hpfvar</em> and <em>lpfvar </em>instance specifications are required to determine the cut-off frequency for the signal filters. The value of slot <em>g </em>in <em>ampfr</em> instance specification<em> </em>is required to determine the gain factor of the <em>Amplifier</em> block. The value of slot <em>amp</em> in the ss instance specification determines the amplitude of the generated signal.</p></li><li><p>Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the two filter part properties and amplifier part property (parts <em>hPF</em>, <em>lPF</em> and<em> a</em>) in the <em>SignalProcessor</em> internal block diagram. Instance specifications <em>hpfvar</em>, <em>lpfvar </em>and<em> ampfr  </em>are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties <em>hPF</em>, <em>lPF</em> and<em> a,</em> respectively. These specifications assign an initial value for the unit-less variable<em> x</em> in each part that is required for filtering the signal in the signal processor at the start of the simulation. But as the simulation runs, these values do change.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="containment_tree_signal_model.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243970863 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05197: Default templates

- page_id: `243970863`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243970863/Default+templates
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:00.777+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Generating Reports from Report Wizard
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are 12 default templates that come with the report engine.

1. Class Specification Report describes the specification of class, interface, and enumeration type of the project.
2. Data Dictionary Report enables you to print elements of data dictionary.
3. IEEE 1233 the standard template based on the IEEE 1233 recommendation.
4. Model Extension describes common UML model extension mechanisms (stereotypes, tagged values, and constraints). It is useful for reporting custom UML profiles.
5. Use Case (Simple) describes system functionalities and actors in a simple format.
6. Use Case (Modern) describes system functionalities and actors that are useful in support of use-case driven analysis.
7. Web Publisher (Simple HTML) enables you to publish models (including diagrams) in simple HTML. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.
8. Web Publisher 2.0 enables you to publish models (including diagrams) in HTML with rich features. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.
9. Activity Diagram provides a standard report for activity diagrams. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes (ordered by node hierarchy in each activity diagram).
10. Activity Diagram Specification provides a standard report for activities. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes in each activity.
11. Sequence Diagram provides a report that shows sequence diagrams and a list of all the messages in the diagrams.
12. Sequence Diagram Specification provides a report that shows sequence diagrams, and a list of all the messages and lifelines in the diagrams.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>There are 12 default templates that come with the report engine.</p><ol><li><strong>Class Specification Report</strong> <br />describes the specification of class, interface, and enumeration type of the project.</li><li><strong>Data Dictionary Report</strong> <br />enables you to print elements of data dictionary.</li><li><strong>IEEE 1233</strong> <br />the standard template based on the IEEE 1233 recommendation.</li><li><strong>Model Extension</strong> <br />describes common UML model extension mechanisms (stereotypes, tagged values, and constraints). It is useful for reporting custom UML profiles.</li><li><strong>Use Case (Simple)</strong> <br />describes system functionalities and actors in a simple format.</li><li><strong>Use Case (Modern)</strong> <br />describes system functionalities and actors that are useful in support of use-case driven analysis.</li><li><strong>Web Publisher (Simple HTML)</strong> <br />enables you to publish models (including diagrams) in simple HTML. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.</li><li><strong>Web Publisher 2.0</strong> <br />enables you to publish models (including diagrams) in HTML with rich features. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.</li><li><strong>Activity Diagram</strong> <br />provides a standard report for activity diagrams. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes (ordered by node hierarchy in each activity diagram).</li><li><strong>Activity Diagram Specification</strong> <br />provides a standard report for activities. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes in each activity.</li><li><strong>Sequence Diagram</strong> <br />provides a report that shows sequence diagrams and a list of all the messages in the diagrams.</li><li><strong>Sequence Diagram Specification</strong> <br />provides a report that shows sequence diagrams, and a list of all the messages and lifelines in the diagrams.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561153 space=MT version=6 -->
## PAGE 05199: Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram

- page_id: `249561153`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561153/Defining+Blocks+in+Block+Definition+Diagram
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:56:35.837+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Introduction

When describing your system structure, you should start by defining [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] in a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='']. Blocks represent the system hierarchy in terms of systems and subsystems. You can model either the logical or physical decomposition of a system, and the specification of software, hardware, or human elements.

The notation for a Block is a rectangle with the stereotype «block» preceding the name. Blocks are the elements of definition because they have a name only. The figure below illustrates the vehicle structure of defined Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='VehicleStructure.mdzip'] sample model.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The vehicle structure in a SysML Block Definition Diagram.

##### Creating Blocks

You can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] in a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] in the following ways:

44

###### Use the Create Element dialog in the Model Browser

You can create all elements including [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] from the shortcut menu.

To create a Block in the Model Browser

1. In the Model Browser, right-click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space=''] in which you want to store the new Block.
2. Do one of the following: - From the shortcut menu, select Create Element . - Press Ctrl+Shift+E.
3. In the Create Element dialog, select Block.
4. Type its name. The new Block is created in the Model Browser. You can represent it in the SysML Block Definition Diagram by dragging it from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] onto the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] .

###### Use the palette on the diagram pane

You can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] directly on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] pane. The Blocks created on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] are created in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''], too.

To create a Block on the diagram pane

1. Open the existing SysML Block Definition Diagram or create a new one. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a diagram' space='']>]]>
2. Do one of the following: - From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] , select the Block button. - Press B.
3. Click on the diagram pane.
4. Type its name. The new Block is created in the Model Browser and represented in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] .

###### Paste a list in the Containment tree or on a diagram pane

You can create Blocks by copying a listed text from other resources (e.g. Word, Excel, HTML, etc.) and pasting it either in the Containment tree or diagram pane.

Detailed procedures are provided in the [Creating elements from other resources](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Creating+elements+from+other+resources) page.

###### Drag .fmu file on the diagram pane

You can represent any model components that are exported to Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI) standard (.fmu files) as Blocks in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''].

The SysML Plugin supports FMI 1.0 and 2.0 versions.

To represent an .fmu file as the Block

1. Drag the .fmu file onto the Block Definition Diagram pane. The Block with name, value properties, their types, and applied fmu stereotype is created. [ATTACHMENT filename='drag_fmu_file.png']

You can execute one fmu Block or co-simulate cooperated fmu Blocks using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation' space='MMA'].

##### Creating Association Block

You can use an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='MED'] to show decomposition of the connector similar to the way that blocks show the decomposition of parts. You can create an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='MED'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block with Owned Ends' space='MED'] between two [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='']. You can represent Association Block usages in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Association Block as Connector type' space='']setting Association Block as Connector type.

To create an Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends

1. Create two Blocks on a diagram pane.
2. From the diagram palette, select Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends button.
3. On the diagram pane, select a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] to be used as the source.
4. Select a target by either selecting an existing Block on the diagram pane, or by clicking on empty space on the diagram to create the target Block. An Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends is created between the source and target [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Participant Property' space='MED'] are created for the Association Block automatically and shown in its references [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v1 specific compartments' space=''] area.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Participant Property' space='MED'] are created automatically after an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='MED'] is created between [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'].

The following figure shows two Association Blocks: *Water Delivery* and *Plumbing*. The connection between the *Spigot Bank* Block and *Faucet* Blocks is decomposed into the *Water Delivery* Block by creating an Association Block. The suppliedByInLink and deliveredToInLink Participant Properties are created and shown in the compartment area. The same is shown with the *Plumbling* Association Block. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='WaterSupply_19.0.mdzip']sample model.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Water Delivery and Plumbing Association Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.

##### Relating Blocks

You can connect Blocks by using these main relationships:

- 
- 
- 

**genGeneralization**

The Generalization relationship conveys an inheritance between Blocks. It is usually used to create a hierarchy in your system. The notation is a solid line with a hollow, triangular arrowhead on the end.

The figure below shows that the *DC Power Supply*, *Inverted Pendulum System*, and *Computer* Blocks are types of the *AC Device* Block. All of the subtypes (*DC Power Supply*, *Inverted Pendulum System*, and *Computer*) require all the characteristics of *AC Device* but add their own specialized characteristics as well. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='InvertedPendulum.mdzip'] sample model. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generalization relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.

**compDirect Composition or Composition**

The Direct Composition or Composition relationships convey a structural decomposition of Blocks. The notation is a solid line between two Blocks with a solid diamond on the composite end.

The following figure shows that the *Wheel* is composed of *Tire* and *Brake*. The Composition can be represented in two different ways: by showing Composition relations between Blocksor by showing Part Properties on a decomposed Block [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v1 specific compartments' space='']. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='VehicleStructure.mdzip'] sample model. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Direct Composition relationship between Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.

**assocDirect Association or Association**

The Direct Association or Association conveysthat a connection can exist between those Blocks. Those Blocks can access each other for some purpose across the connection. The Direct Association notation isa solid line with an arrowhead on the end, while the Association notation consists only of asolid line.

The figure below illustrates the Association between the*Wheel* and *WirelessTirePressureMonitor* Blocks. The name of the Association is *BandMount,* which describes the type of connection that could exist between the wheel and wireless tire pressure monitor*.* The Association can be represented in two different ways: by showing the Association relation between Blocks or by showing the Reference Property on a decomposed Block [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v1 specific compartments' space='']. The figure**is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='hybrid sport utility vehicle.mdzip'] sample model.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Association relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.

For how to create, remove, change a style, route, create a line jump, insert a shape on a path, or manage path compartments, read the [Working with paths and relationships](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Working+with+paths+and+relationships) page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Introduction</h3><p>When describing your system structure, you should start by defining <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Block Definition Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Blocks <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">represent the system hierarchy in terms of systems and subsystems. You can model either the logical or physical decomposition of a system, and the specification of software, hardware, or human elements.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The notation for a Block is a rectangle with the stereotype «block» preceding the name. Blocks are the elements of definition because they have a name only. The figure below illustrates the vehicle structure of defined Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram. The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="VehicleStructure.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model.</span></p><p><ac:image You can represent Association Block usages in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Internal Block Diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> by <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Association Block as Connector type" /><ac:link-body>setting <span class="current">Association Block as Connector type</span></ac:link-body></ac:link><span class="current">.</span></p><p>To create an Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="63e324da-28c4-42c6-8159-b96a719e8233">Create two Blocks on a diagram pane.</li><li data-uuid="9ff02ab6-31af-4f0b-8457-b4a176ddf098">From the diagram palette, select <strong>Association Block </strong>or <strong>Association Block with Owned Ends</strong> button.</li><li data-uuid="c0220e27-4789-4cba-adf1-1bdee228056d">On the diagram pane, select a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /></ac:link> to be used as the source.</li><li data-uuid="66b0291d-9d18-453d-b014-96f79502ae9b">Select a target by either selecting an existing Block on the diagram pane, or by clicking on empty space on the diagram to create the target Block.<br />An Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends is created between the source and target <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Participant Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Participant Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are created for the Association Block automatically and shown in its <em>references</em> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML v1 specific compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[compartment ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>area.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0cc59ef3-68ec-4b55-ba00-58d27118c434"><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Participant Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Participant properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are created automatically after an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Association Block" /></ac:link> is created between <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following figure shows two Association Blocks: <em>Water Delivery</em> and <em>Plumbing</em>. The connection between the <em>Spigot Bank</em> Block and <em>Faucet</em> Blocks is decomposed into the <em>Water Delivery</em> Block by creating an Association Block. The suppliedByInLink and deliveredToInLink Participant Properties are created and shown in the compartment area. The same is shown with the <em>Plumbling</em> Association Block. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="WaterSupply_19.0.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Association Block as Connector type" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> sample model.</span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="association_block.png" /></ac:image>Water Delivery and Plumbing Association Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><h3>Relating Blocks</h3><p>You can connect Blocks by using these main relationships:</p><ul><li data-uuid="582d94bc-aa90-4122-9112-3c1e81b2ad00"><ac:link ac:anchor="gen"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Generalization]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="e2e5cdd0-6e5d-44be-b0f5-f5b196a5ca40"><ac:link ac:anchor="comp"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Direct Composition or Composition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="0c96078a-dfc4-4e73-be42-c235ca488d5d"><ac:link ac:anchor="assoc"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Direct Association or Association]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><strong><br /></strong></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c401d354-28d1-42da-ba33-c036bbb91f64"><ac:parameter ac:name="">gen</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Generalization</strong></p><p>The Generalization relationship conveys an inheritance between Blocks. It is usually used to create a hierarchy in your system. The notation is a solid line with a hollow, triangular arrowhead on the end.</p><p>The figure below shows that the <em>DC Power Supply</em>, <em>Inverted Pendulum System</em>, and <em>Computer</em> Blocks are types of the <em>AC Device</em> Block. All of the subtypes (<em>DC Power Supply</em>, <em>Inverted Pendulum System</em>, and <em>Computer</em>) require all the characteristics of <em>AC Device</em> but add their own specialized characteristics as well. The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="InvertedPendulum.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model. <br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="generalization_relationship.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Generalization relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d25a63e-1920-4a0d-841b-a8f28efb2f53"><ac:parameter ac:name="">comp</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Direct Composition or Composition</strong></p><p>The Direct Composition or Composition relationships convey a structural decomposition of Blocks. The notation is a solid line between two Blocks with a solid diamond on the composite end.</p><p>The following figure shows that the <em>Wheel</em> is composed of <em>Tire</em> and <em>Brake</em>. The Composition can be represented in two different ways: by showing Composition relations <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">between Blocks </span>or by showing Part Properties on a decomposed Block <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML v1 specific compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[compartment]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="VehicleStructure.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="composition_relationship.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#707070);">Direct Composition relationship between Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.</span></h6><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11ef6e36-5f30-49dd-b898-43b8f534276d"><ac:parameter ac:name="">assoc</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Direct Association or Association</strong></p><p>The Direct Association or Association conveys<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> that a connection can exist between those Blocks.</span> Those Blocks can access each other for some purpose across the connection. The Direct Association notation is<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> a solid line with an arrowhead on the end, while the Association notation consists only of a<span> solid line. </span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The figure below illustrates the Association between the </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><em>Wheel</em> and <em>WirelessTirePressureMonitor</em> Blocks. The name of the Association is <em>BandMount,</em> which describes the type of connection that could exist between the wheel and wireless tire pressure monitor<em>.</em> The Association can be represented in two different ways: by showing the Association relation between Blocks or by showing the Reference Property on a decomposed Block <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML v1 specific compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[compartment]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The figure<em> </em>is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="hybrid sport utility vehicle.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model. </span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="association_relationship.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Association relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><p><br /></p><p>For how to create, remove, change a style, route, create a line jump, insert a shape on a path, or manage path compartments, read the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Working+with+paths+and+relationships" class="current">Working with paths and relationships</a> page.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561599 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05200: Defining Constraint Blocks

- page_id: `249561599`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561599/Defining+Constraint+Blocks
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-08T17:36:24.903+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling parametric constraints
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

With a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='MED'], you can define parameters needed to calculate a formula. In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space=''], Constraint Blocks are used as types of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space='MED']. You can use the same Constraint Block in several Contexts. Usually, constraints are stored in a library, where you will be able to export and reuse them in other models.

To create a Constraint Block

1. Open a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space=''] .
2. Click the Constraint Block button on the diagram palette, then click an empty space on the diagram pane. An unnamed constraint block is created.
3. Type a Constraint Block name directly on the shape of that element and press Enter.
4. In a constraints compartment, type a formula.
5. Create parameters. Click the Constraint Block. The smart manipulator appears. Click the Parse and Create Parameters . [ATTACHMENT filename='parse_and_create_parameters.png']
6. According to the formula, parameters having default types assigned are created. If necessary, you may change the type of the particular parameter. [ATTACHMENT filename='constraint_block.png']

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parameters and properties' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>With a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Constraint Block" /></ac:link>, you can define parameters needed to calculate a formula. In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Parametric Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Parametric diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, Constraint Blocks are used as types of <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Constraint Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Constraint Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. You can use the same Constraint Block in several Contexts. Usually, constraints are stored in a library, where you will be able to export and reuse them in other models.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a Constraint Block</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="5a62ee78-1e94-45ac-8783-b621e93be28d">Open a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create a new one]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li data-uuid="53efb7a1-3aa4-4758-84b2-abc613f39ce0">Click the <strong>Constraint Block</strong> button on the diagram palette, then click an empty space on the diagram pane. An unnamed constraint block is created.</li><li data-uuid="7b9059c1-abe2-4890-805f-5f2ef7753af1">Type a Constraint Block name <span>directly </span><span>on the shape of that element and press Enter.</span></li><li data-uuid="f2c67f58-5932-4c7b-b2f8-75b64929e857">In a constraints compartment, type a formula.</li><li data-uuid="5dca0941-de85-4143-b878-9d72c7493c1f">Create parameters. Click the Constraint Block. The smart manipulator appears. Click the <strong>Parse and Create Parameters</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="parse_and_create_parameters.png" /></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="567e626d-ba67-49f2-a479-dd5b277de2f3">According to the formula, parameters having default types assigned are created. If necessary, you may change the type of the particular parameter.<br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="149"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraint_block.png" /></ac:image></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="59b7a99c-7a3b-444f-aa1a-4e8d25a22a23"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="bf86b750-7306-4ba5-872a-d04f4062ae85"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying parameters and properties" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="636e0775-4048-4dd8-858c-d126b45fd6d7"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969603 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05201: Defining Constraint condition

- page_id: `243969603`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969603/Defining+Constraint+condition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:33.477+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Validation > Custom validation > Creating new validation rules
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

You must specify the validation condition for a validation rule. This condition must be true when evaluated in order for the Constraint to be satisfied. The result of a validation rule must be of the boolean type.

To define a Constraint condition

1. Open the Constraint Specification window. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']>]]>
2. Find the Specification property and select its value box.
3. Click the [...] button. The Specification dialog opens.
4. From the Language list, select one of the following languages:
  1. OCL 2.0 is used for validation rules specified in the OCL language. **OCL Header**If you select the OCL2.0 language, the header of the expression from the constraint information is generated automatically according to the following rules: context <constrained element> <constraint type> <constraint name if any>:. See the following image.[IMAGE alt='' src='']**Constraint types**Since the Constraint is stereotyped by «validationRule» which is derived from «invariant » stereotype, inv is shown in the header. Only inv constraints can be evaluated. Other types of Constraints are not evaluated, but can be modeled for documentation purposes:**def**– for the expression of the constraint with «definition» stereotype applied.**init, derive**- for the expression of the default value of the property.The **derive** expressions can be evaluated indirectly when the validation rule (**inv** constraint) is referencing the property and the validation rule is evaluated.**pre, post, body**- for the expression of the appropriate fields of operation.**OCL Performance**When evaluating the validation rule defined in OCL language, the validation on the first run can have a delay of 20-30 seconds (depending on the computer performance) while the Java compiler is loading. Subsequent validations will run faster than the first one.If the validation process is run heavily on medium-large projects, increasing the default Java VM size is advisable. By default, the VM size is set to 400MB in the modeling tool; increasing this to 600 (or 800 if the computer has sufficient RAM) might improve the performance.
  2. Binary is used for more advanced expressions not easily expressed in OCL. These expressions are written in Java, compiled, and specified in the modeling tool's classpath. These expressions can then be specified as validation rule expressions.
  3. Scripts of JavaScript, Jython, Groovy , and BeanShell . Learn more about script writing in Creating executable opaque behaviors. Since constraints cannot have parameters, you may skip the information about managing parameters.
  4. StructuredExpression . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='']>]]>
5. In the Body box, type the expression of the selected language syntax. The modeling tool can evaluate only those validation rules whose expression is defined in one of the languages listed above. The other languages can be used only for documentation purposes. You can add hyperlinks in the **Body**box. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks' space='']>]]>You can define expressions for global validation rules. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Global validation rules' space='']>]]>
6. (Optional) Click the Evaluation Mode button to execute an expression on the actual testing model while editing. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expression Evaluation' space='']>]]>
7. Click OK . The Constraint condition is defined.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4de8cb5d-9a7e-4bca-a25e-64130f3d8b80"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You must specify the validation condition for a validation rule. This condition must be true when evaluated in order for the Constraint to be satisfied. The result of a validation rule must be of the boolean type.</p><p>To define a Constraint condition</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="0d414318-ad9e-488e-bc9a-41fffdc5fdba">Open the Constraint <strong>Specification </strong>window. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to open the Specification window >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="e3708937-22c5-469b-970d-3814b5a9080c">Find the <strong>Specification</strong> property and select its value box.</li><li data-uuid="12ed1533-338d-494f-b6da-ae1d28864d3c">Click the <strong>[...]</strong> button. The <strong>Specification </strong>dialog opens.</li><li data-uuid="1b8950eb-a9bc-4c91-96e4-06bc4f50f564">From the <strong>Language </strong>list, select one of the following languages:<ol><li><strong>OCL 2.0</strong> is used for validation rules specified in the OCL language.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33001904-6d9f-4343-96f1-bdbc9e0de79b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>OCL Header </strong></p><p>If you select the OCL2.0 language, the header of the expression from the constraint information is generated automatically according to the following rules: context &lt;constrained element&gt; &lt;constraint type&gt; &lt;constraint name if any&gt;:.  See the following image.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification.png" /></ac:image></p><p><strong>Constraint types </strong></p><p>Since the Constraint is stereotyped by «validationRule» which is derived from «invariant » stereotype, inv is shown in the header. Only inv constraints can be evaluated. Other types of Constraints are not evaluated, but can be modeled for documentation purposes:</p><ul><li><strong>def </strong>– for the expression of the constraint with «definition» stereotype applied.</li><li><strong>init, derive </strong>- for the expression of the default value of the property.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9d0ff826-a8f7-41fd-ae64-7fd15ed1b889"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>derive</strong> expressions can be evaluated indirectly when the validation rule (<strong>inv</strong> constraint) is referencing the property and the validation rule is evaluated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><strong>pre, post, body </strong>- for the expression of the appropriate fields of operation. </li></ul><p><strong>OCL Performance </strong></p><p>When evaluating the validation rule defined in OCL language, the validation on the first run can have a delay of 20-30 seconds (depending on the computer performance) while the Java compiler is loading. Subsequent validations will run faster than the first one. </p><p>If the validation process is run heavily on medium-large projects, increasing the default Java VM size is advisable. By default, the VM size is set to 400MB in the modeling tool; increasing this to 600 (or 800 if the computer has sufficient RAM) might improve the performance.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><strong>Binary</strong> is used for more advanced expressions not easily expressed in OCL. These expressions are written in Java, compiled, and specified in the modeling tool's classpath. These expressions can then be specified as validation rule expressions.</li><li>Scripts of <strong>JavaScript, Jython, Groovy</strong>, and <strong>BeanShell</strong>. Learn more about script writing in Creating executable opaque behaviors. Since constraints cannot have parameters, you may skip the information about managing parameters.</li><li><strong>StructuredExpression</strong>. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying criteria for querying model" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about specifying criteria for querying model >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="2bb31874-dfb6-4fc7-ae8d-a48162e06109">In the <strong>Body </strong>box, type the expression of the selected language syntax.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b86ab07-bc39-41d5-8eb4-c3080e72e935"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The modeling tool can evaluate only those validation rules whose expression is defined in one of the languages listed above. The other languages can be used only for documentation purposes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a99b9755-0acf-444c-ad9c-5ea350f97a74"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can add hyperlinks in the <strong>Body </strong>box. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining hyperlinks" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to define hyperlinks >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p>You can define expressions for global validation rules. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Global validation rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about global validation rules >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="b71296d5-8848-4dcc-911c-50147c238894">(Optional) Click the <strong>Evaluation Mode </strong>button to execute an expression on the actual testing model while editing. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Expression Evaluation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to use the Evaluation Mode >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="762fcca7-e520-4e7f-a418-373ea93ea146">Click <strong>OK</strong>. The Constraint condition is defined.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968355 space=MT version=10 -->
## PAGE 05202: Defining hyperlinks

- page_id: `243968355`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968355/Defining+hyperlinks
- version_number: 10
- version_date: `2025-09-11T19:46:22.036+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Manage Navigation
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The hyperlink is a reference to another location that you access by clicking a highlighted text or element. Hyperlinks ensure fast access to information directly from your model. You can create hyperlinks in text, on elements, or on the entire diagram to link them to external or internal resources. For example, a hyperlink can open a file from your file system. The following figure illustrates all available hyperlink types.

The hyperlinks to OSLC resources are available only if the Cameo DataHub Plugin is installed. Please see[how to install the Cameo DataHub Plugin >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=installing-plugins&version=latest)

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Hyperlink types in modeling tools.

##### Hyperlinks representation

Visual representation of hyperlinks is outlined below:

##### Adding hyperlinks

For more information on how to add and manage hyperlinks, please see:

- Linking model elements to OSLC resources

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manage Navigation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='HTML editor' space='']
- Managing hyperlinks

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>The hyperlink is a reference to another location that you access by clicking a highlighted text or element. Hyperlinks ensure fast access to information directly from your model. You can create hyperlinks in text, on elements, or on the entire diagram to link them to external or internal resources. For example, a hyperlink can open a file from your file system. The following figure illustrates all available hyperlink types.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="adccf501-3612-4cd8-a12d-2f4cdd547da8"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The hyperlinks to OSLC resources are available only if the Cameo DataHub Plugin is installed. Please see<a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=installing-plugins&amp;version=latest"> how to install the Cameo DataHub Plugin &gt;&gt;</a></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:queryparams="effects=border-simple,shadow-kn"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlinks_illustration.png" /></ac:image>Hyperlink types in modeling tools.</h6><h3>Hyperlinks representation</h3><p>Visual representation of hyperlinks is outlined below:</p><ul><ul><li data-uuid="c99a3d53-18ba-4059-ba16-0c55531485d5">Hyperlink in text is underlined and blue. For example, <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="12"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_in_text_.png" /></ac:image>.</li><li data-uuid="852e4b4a-7910-4877-82ec-30f37c5b3e3e"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Hyperlink on element or diagram:<br />         - In the Model Browser, the <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlinkElementAdornment.gif" /></ac:image></span> icon appears on the element. For example, <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="16"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_on_element_in_model_browser.png" /></ac:image>, <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_on_diagram_in_model_browser.png" /></ac:image>.<br />         - On the diagram pane, on the symbol, the icon representing the subject to which the hyperlink is added appears. For example:<br />                      - To a webpage:<br />                                 <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="59"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_webpage.png" /></ac:image><br />                      - To a file:<br />                                   <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="69"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_pdf.png" /></ac:image><br />                      - To an element or diagram:<br />                                     <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="48"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_ibd.png" /></ac:image> <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="49"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_block.png" /></ac:image><br />                      - To OSLC resources, wizards, validation, and reports:<br />                                      <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="65"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_oslc_wizards_validation_reports.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a98e1b75-a0ea-4c29-a2e2-1c8b6c7d9669"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">If the element has an active navigation target then an active navigation target icon is show on a symbol. You can choose whether to show the icon by changing a value of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Customizing environment options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Environment Option]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <strong>Show Active Navigation Icons</strong>. By default, this option is set to <em>true</em>. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></ul><h3>Adding hyperlinks</h3><p class="confluence-link"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">For more information on how to add and manage hyperlinks, please see:</span></p><p class="confluence-link"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d690d70d-4629-4f6c-8611-6e7bccf70846" /></p><ul><li data-uuid="260ef43b-7f26-4819-a4ba-7c9bc6c56027"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CDH/?contextKey=linking-model-elements-to-oslc-resources&amp;version=latest">Linking model elements to OSLC resources</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="28e11221-92f4-42b4-8f3f-7d4fffcc8ebb"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Manage Navigation" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="2871b59a-d745-47da-b3ee-de84a5e4a868"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with model elements" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="ea18120d-1b2d-4e4e-8361-58c84789827a"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="HTML editor" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="86a9c696-a1e4-4ba2-8d40-ffe27665bf68"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/DEVG/?contextKey=managing-hyperlinks&amp;version=latest">Managing hyperlinks</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561272 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 05203: Defining Interface Blocks

- page_id: `249561272`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561272/Defining+Interface+Blocks
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:10:55.661+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks > Defining interfaces using ports
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1332798826

1332798829

1332798827

To define Interface Block

1. Create Interface Block in the model in one of the following way:
  - F rom the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''], select the**Interface Block** button, and click the on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''].
  - In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''], right-click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='']in which you want to store your interfaces, and select **Create Element** > **Interface Block**.
2. Specify Flow Properties in one of the following way:
  - Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] of Interface Block, select Properties group, click the Create button, and select Flow Property. Type its name, specify its direction, other properties, and click Close .
  - In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''], right-click the Interface Block, and select **Create Element** > **Flow Property**. Type its name, specify its direction, or other properties.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Defined Interface Block and specified its Flow Properties in the Model Browser and Specification window.

1332798825

**Related pages**

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **Inverted****Pendulum** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[InvertedPendulum.mdzip](https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/9919520/InvertedPendulum.mdzip?version=1&modificationDate=1498477335401&api=v2).*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\Inverted Pendulum\Inverted Pendulum.mdzip.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Interface Block</strong>.</span></span></li></ul></li><li><span class="confluence-link">Specify Flow Properties <span class="confluence-link"> in <span class="confluence-link">one of</span> the following way:</span></span><ul><li>Open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of Interface Block, select <strong>Properties</strong> group, click the <strong>Create </strong>button, and select Flow Property. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561242 space=MT version=5 -->
## PAGE 05204: Defining interfaces using ports

- page_id: `249561242`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561242/Defining+interfaces+using+ports
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-08T18:33:43.091+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: ['conjugate', 'is_conjugated', 'interface', 'ports', 'mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Ports are points at which external entities can connect to and interact with a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] in different or more limited ways than connecting directly to the Block itself. They are properties with a type that specifies features available to the external entities via [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED'] to the ports. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space='MED'] define the boundary by specifying which features of the owning [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='MED'] are visible through external connectors, while [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space='MED'] define the boundary with their own features. Proxy Ports are always typed by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space='MED'], a specialized kind of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] that has no behaviors or internal parts. Full Ports cannot be behavioral in the UML sense of standing in for the owning object, because they handle features themselves, rather than exposing features of their owners, or internal parts of their owners.

In the following figure, the *chanel2* Proxy Port is typed by *GrayCodeIB* Interface Block which contains two Flow Properties of *Pulse* Signal:*out a* and *out b*. It means the Proxy Port that is typed by *GrayCodeIB* Interface Block can send and receive Flow Items of *Pulse* Signal.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The GrayCodeIB Interface Block is a type of Proxy Ports.

To define interfaces using ports

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] and decompose it in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram' space='']>]]> [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Decomposing Blocks' space='']>]]>
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']SysML Internal Block Diagram , create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space='MED'] . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating ports' space='']>]]>
3. Create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space='MED'] in the model and specify its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space='MED'] . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Interface Blocks' space='']>]]>
4. Select create Interface Block as Proxy Port type. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting port type' space='']>]]>

If ports are connected via a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED'], both port types must be compatible.

If the same [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space='MED'] is set as the type for ports on both ends, one of the ports should be conjugated. The conjugate mechanism reverses the direction of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space='MED'] and makes the connection compatible. For this, you can conjugate a port or create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conjugated Interface Block' space='MED'].

To conjugate a port, do one of the following:

- On the diagram pane, right-click the port shape, and select the Is Conjugated command.
- Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] of port, and set the Is Conjugated property value to true. The port is conjugated and the tilde symbol (~) appears before the port's type. See the following figure. [ATTACHMENT filename='conjugated_port.png']

To create a Conjugated Interface Block

- Right-click one or more Interface Blocks and select Tools > Create Conjugated Interface Block . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conjugated Interface Block' space='MED'] is created with the same [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Directed Feature' space='MED'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space='MED'] as the original but with opposite directions. It is created under the same owner as the original Interface Block.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **Inverted****Pendulum** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='InvertedPendulum.mdzip'].
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\Inverted Pendulum\Inverted Pendulum.mdzip.
