<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966441 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05222: Deployment diagram

- page_id: `243966441`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966441/Deployment+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:02.387+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: ['implementation', 'implementation-diagram', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308620457

308620459

308620458

**Overview**

A Deployment diagram falls under the structural diagramming family. It focuses on showing the physical layout of various hardware components ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MED']), e.g. CPU, a database server, and an application server that compose a system as well as the distribution of executable programs (software components) on hardware. For instance, to describe a website, the Deployment diagram would show what hardware components exist, what software components run, and how the different pieces are interconnected. Deployment diagrams are crucial when dealing with distributed systems.

**Purpose**

Using a Deployment diagram, you can show the actual computers and devices (Nodes), along with the connections they have to each other, thus specifying a system topology. Inside the Nodes, executable [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space='MED'] and objects are located in a way that it shows where the software units are residing and on which Nodes they are executed. You may also show dependencies between Components.

**Usage**

A Deployment diagram can be used to:

- show the structure of the run-time system
- capture the hardware that is going to be used to implement the system and links between different hardware items
- model physical hardware elements and the communication paths between them
- plan the system architecture
- document the deployment of software components or nodes.

**Summary**

Deployment diagrams are valuable because they can be:

- used to model the hardware platform for a system
- used to identify hardware capabilities that have an impact on the performance planning and software configuration.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Deployment diagram

308620456

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="436734ff-2ba8-4504-b4fb-3ea4e20f3dc9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308620457</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0b7ce33-d2d9-4d2f-b3d8-fec326568fde"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308620459</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="915fce0c-7d72-4a9e-870b-a54415153dcb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308620458</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Overview</strong></p><p>A Deployment diagram falls under the structural diagramming family. It focuses on showing the physical layout of various hardware components (<ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Node" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Nodes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>), e.g. CPU, a database server, and an application server that compose a system as well as the distribution of executable programs (software components) on hardware. For instance, to describe a website, the Deployment diagram would show what hardware components exist, what software components run, and how the different pieces are interconnected. Deployment diagrams are crucial when dealing with distributed systems.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>Using a Deployment diagram, you can show the actual computers and devices (Nodes), along with the connections they have to each other, thus specifying a system topology. Inside the Nodes, executable <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Component" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Components]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and objects are located in a way that it shows where the software units are residing and on which Nodes they are executed. You may also show dependencies between Components.</p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>A Deployment diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>show the structure of the run-time system</li><li>capture the hardware that is going to be used to implement the system and links between different hardware items</li><li>model physical hardware elements and the communication paths between them</li><li>plan the system architecture</li><li>document the deployment of software components or nodes. </li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Deployment diagrams are valuable because they can be:</p><ul><li>used to model the hardware platform for a system</li><li>used to identify hardware capabilities that have an impact on the performance planning and software configuration. </li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deployment_diagram_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Deployment diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="292ab185-bc83-4d52-bf5f-8dc8aad28c4b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308620456</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973856 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05223: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

- page_id: `243973856`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973856/Deprecated+JavaScript+Nashorn
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:24:43.752+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Macro engine > Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

965417895

INLINE

965417916

INLINE

965417905

INLINE

The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. You can learn more about deprecated JavaScript Nashorn [here](https://openjdk.java.net/jeps/335). Please use JavaScript Rhino instead. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='JavaScript migration from Nashorn to Rhino engine' space=''] page.

All JavaScript Nashorn usages need to be changed to JavaScript Rhino in projects to work correctly. Below are the recommended steps for achieving that:

1. Use one of the following options to run the modeling tool:
  - Proceeding with this option means that none of your project's JavaScript Nashorn scripts will work until they are changed to or rewritten in JavaScript Rhino.
  - Proceeding with this option means that your project's JavaScript Nashorn scripts will be executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine; thus, the majority of the scripts will work, but they will still need to be changed to or rewritten in JavaScript Rhino.
2. 
3. 

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Diagram showing the JavaScript Nashorn usages' change to JavaScript Rhino.

##### Running the modeling tool

###### Option 1. Run modeling tool without executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts (default)

Following this option means:

- The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are not executed, meaning that the model will not function as expected due to script failures.
- This is the default option; simply run the modeling tool and open a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed.

###### Option 2. Run modeling tool executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts with JavaScript Rhino engine

Following this option means:

- The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine. Due to the similarities between Rhino and Nashorn, most of the project's scripts are likely to work.
- To proceed with this option, you need to add an additional system property, as described below.

To enable JavaScript Nashorn script execution with JavaScript Rhino engine

1. Open the <modeling tool installation directory>\bin\<modeling tool name>.properties file.
2. Locate the JAVA_ARGS= line
3. Add the following system property: -Dexecute.legacy.javascript.as.rhino=true
4. Restart the modeling tool.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, the 'JavaScript Nashorn (deprecated)' error notification is displayed.

##### Opening a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages

Opening a project that contains JavaScript *Nashorn* scripts immediately displays a notification about the removal of the JavaScript *Nashorn* scripting language. The notification is displayed only once upon the first opening of the project with the new version of the tool. You can click the **Find Usages**link to run the **Deprecated JavaScript** validation suite to locate all usages of JavaScript Nashorn (for more information, see the section below)*.*

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Finding and resolving JavaScript Nashorn**usages**

The recommended steps to find and resolve JavaScript Nashorn usages:

1. Run the Deprecated JavaScript validation suite to .
2. Evaluate each script failing the validation and rewrite it in JavaScript Rhino if necessary. Due to the similarities between JavaScript Rhino and JavaScript Nashorn, most of the project's Nashorn scripts are likely to work with the Rhino engine. However, a review is recommended as to whether the Nashorn scripts' execution with the Rhino engine are accurate. If not, the Nashorn script must be modified to work in Rhino.
3. via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino**solver.

###### Finding JavaScript Nashorn**usages

To locate all usages of JavaScript Nashorn in a project, we recommend running the**Deprecated JavaScript**validation suite (**Analyze**>**Validation**>**Validate**). The validation suite contains the **Deprecated JavaScript** validation rule, which:

- detects all JavaScript Nashorn**usages;
- allows you to change the JavaScript Nashorn**language to JavaScript Rhino**for implemented expressions via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino**solver.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### After running the **Deprecated JavaScript** validation suite, hold Ctrl+A to select all detected instances of JavaScript *Nashorn* and resolve them via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino** solver in the Validation Results panel.

For a detailed description of the validation rule, see the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript' space='']** page.

###### Resolving JavaScript Nashorn**usages

The **Change to JavaScript Rhino**solver changes the used scripting language without affecting the script itself. In many cases, changing the used scripting language will not affect the functionality of the script. Otherwise, the 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification (shown below) will appear, requiring you to rewrite the script in JavaScript Rhino.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76c40243-6bcb-4aa7-982c-d6bf34474681"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">965417895</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df36ab11-b297-44d1-831d-bfa188e162ca"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">965417916</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3947d9fd-b60f-4e41-aee4-b0123a7f849b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">965417905</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="823a0d75-ba18-4882-8e57-2f5a7658ce80"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. You can learn more about deprecated JavaScript Nashorn <a href="https://openjdk.java.net/jeps/335">here</a>. Please use JavaScript Rhino instead. For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="JavaScript migration from Nashorn to Rhino engine" /></ac:link> page<span>.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All JavaScript Nashorn usages need to be changed to JavaScript Rhino in projects to work correctly. Below are the recommended steps for achieving that:</p><ol><li>Use one of the following options to run the modeling tool:<ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Option 1. Run modeling tool without executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts (default)" /><br />Proceeding with this option means that none of your project's JavaScript Nashorn scripts will work until they are changed to or rewritten in JavaScript Rhino.  </li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Option 2. Run modeling tool executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts with JavaScript Rhino engine" /><br />Proceeding with this option means that your project's JavaScript Nashorn scripts will be executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine; thus, the majority of the scripts will work, but they will still need to be changed to or rewritten in JavaScript Rhino.  </li></ul></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Finding and resolving JavaScript Nashorn usages"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Find and resolve JavaScript Nashorn usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Nashron_to_Rhino.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Diagram showing the JavaScript Nashorn usages' change to JavaScript Rhino.</h6><h3>Running the modeling tool</h3><h4>Option 1. Run modeling tool without executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts (default)</h4><p>Following this option means:</p><ul><li>The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are not executed, meaning that the model will not function as expected due to script failures.</li><li>This is the default option; simply run the modeling tool and open a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages.</li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="expression_evaluation_error.png" /></ac:image>For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed.</h6><h4>Option 2. Run modeling tool executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts with JavaScript Rhino engine</h4><p>Following this option means:</p><ul><li>The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine. Due to the similarities between Rhino and Nashorn, most of the project's scripts are likely to work.</li><li>To proceed with this option, you need to add an additional system property, as described below.</li></ul><p>To enable JavaScript Nashorn script execution with JavaScript Rhino engine</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\bin\&lt;modeling tool name&gt;.properties</em> file.</li><li>Locate the <em>JAVA_ARGS=</em> line</li><li>Add the following system property:<br /><em>-Dexecute.legacy.javascript.as.rhino=true</em></li><li><span>Restart the modeling tool.</span></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Deprecated_Nashorn_error.png" /></ac:image>For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, the 'JavaScript Nashorn (deprecated)' error notification is displayed.</h6><h3>Opening a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages</h3><p>Opening a project that contains JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> scripts immediately displays a notification about the removal of the JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> scripting language. The notification is displayed only once upon the first opening of the project with the new version of the tool. You can click the <strong>Find Usages </strong>link to run the <strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong> validation suite to locate all usages of JavaScript Nashorn (for more information, see the <ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Finding and resolving JavaScript Nashorn usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> section below)<em>.</em></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Discontinued_Nashorn_notification.png" /></ac:image></p><h3><strong>Finding and resolving <span>JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>usages</span></strong></h3><p>The recommended steps to find and resolve JavaScript Nashorn usages:</p><ol><li class="auto-cursor-target">Run the <strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong><span> validation suite to <ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[find all JavaScript Nashorn usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><p>Evaluate each script failing the validation and rewrite it in JavaScript Rhino if necessary.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00e77b0b-72b5-4a81-87c2-56b74adc6f65"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Due to the similarities between JavaScript Rhino and JavaScript Nashorn, most of the project's Nashorn scripts are likely to work with the Rhino engine. However, a review is recommended as to whether the Nashorn scripts' execution with the Rhino engine are accurate. If not, the Nashorn script must be modified to work in Rhino.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Resolve the validation error]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> via the <span><strong style="text-align: left;">Change to JavaScript Rhino </strong>solver.</span></li></ol><h4>Finding JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>usages</h4><p class="auto-cursor-target">To locate all <span>usages of JavaScript Nashorn in a project, we recommend running the </span><strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong><span> validation suite (</span><strong>Analyze</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Validation</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Validate</strong><span>). The validation suite contains the <strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong> validation rule, which:</span></p><ul><li><span> detects all JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>usages;</span></li><li><span>allows you to change the JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>language to JavaScript Rhino<em> </em>for implemented expressions via the <strong style="text-align: left;">Change to JavaScript Rhino </strong>solver.</span></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="validation_results.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>After running the <strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong> validation suite, hold Ctrl+A to select all detected instances of JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> and resolve them via the <strong>Change to JavaScript Rhino</strong> solver in the Validation Results panel.</span></h6><p><span>For a detailed description of the validation rule, see the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Deprecated JavaScript" /></ac:link></strong> page.</span></p><h4>Resolving JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>usages</h4><p><span>The <strong style="text-align: left;">Change to JavaScript Rhino </strong>solver changes the used scripting language without affecting the script itself. In many cases, changing the used scripting language will not affect the functionality of the script. Otherwise, the 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification (shown below) will appear, requiring you to rewrite the script in JavaScript Rhino.</span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Expression_evaluation_failure.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562374 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05224: Description of State Machines used in this model

- page_id: `249562374`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562374/Description+of+State+Machines+used+in+this+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:36:02.782+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Humidifier system sample
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In this model, there are two main components that interacted with each other: the room and the humidifier system. The purpose of the humidifier is to control the humidity of the room once a user has selected a humidity level for the room. State machines are used to show the control of humidity in the room, both to depict the user’s input and the humidifier’s control system. For the sake of brevity, the following SysML v1 diagrams form only the core set of diagrams from the SysML v1 model, and include all three state machines used in this example.

1. HumidifierSystem Internal Block Diagram : This diagram is part of the HumidifierSystem block. The flow between the part properties room (typed by HumidifiedRoom ) and humidifier (typed by Humidifier ) are shown to be going through ports. These ports are the interfaces between the two system components, and contain numeric information about the humidity and vapor going between the two components. [ATTACHMENT filename='humidifier_system_ibd.png']
2. Humidifier Internal Block Diagram : This diagram is part of the Humidifier block. The flow within the humidifier system are depicted in this diagram. With information about the room’s current humidity from the *humidity_in* port, and control signal from the humidity control system from the part property control (typed by*Control*), the part property *heaterControl*(typed by *HeaterControl*) uses a state machine to decide whether or not to turn on the heater control system in order to generate vapor in the part property *vaporgenerationplant*(typed by *VaporGenerationPlant*). The part property*vaporgenerationplant* also uses information from the part property control to determine the amount of vapor released into the room (and consumed from the part property*watertank*, typed by*WaterTank*) as well as let *control* know the temperature of water in the vapor generation system. The part property *control* also uses information from when the user wants to turn on the humidifier from the part property *usage* (typed by *UsageScenario*) and information about the amount of water in the water tank from the part property*watertank*. [ATTACHMENT filename='humidifier_ibd.png']
3. UsageScenarioStateMachine State Machine Diagram : This state machine diagram depicts the behavior of the part property usage in the Humidifier internal block diagram and it determines when (as in, at which times) the humidifier system should humidify the room. [ATTACHMENT filename='usage_scenario_stm.png']
4. ControlStateMachine State Machine Diagram : This state machine diagram depicts the behavior of the part property control in the Humidifier internal block diagram and it determines the operation of the heater control system heatercontrol and the vapor generation plant vaporgenerationplant based on information about the water levels in the tank from the watertank , the temperature of vaporization from the vaporgenerationplant , and the operation of when the humidifier system should be started from usage . [ATTACHMENT filename='control_stm.png']
5. HeaterControlSM State Machine Diagram : This state machine diagram depicts the behavior of the part property heaterControl in the Humidifier internal block diagram. It uses information from the room’s current humidity from the humidity_in pin, the target humidity from the part property targetHumidity , and the control signal from control in order to decide whether or not to turn on the heater control system for the generation of vapor with vaporgenerationplant . [ATTACHMENT filename='heater_control_stm.png']
6. VaporGenerationPlant Internal Block Diagram : This internal block diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flow associated with the part property vaporgenerationplant in the Humidifier internal block diagram. Given the control signal from control for its water fan and the control signal from the heaterControl , after the numeric calculations it gives information about the water temperature to the control as well as information about the generated vapor from the watertank and for the room. [ATTACHMENT filename='vaporGenerationPlantCalculation_ibd.png']
7. WaterTank Parametric Diagram : This parametric diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flows associated with the part property watertank in the Humidifier internal block diagram. Given the information about water consumed for vapor generation from vaporgenerationplant , numeric calculations provide information about the volume of remaining water and this new information is sent to the control part property. [ATTACHMENT filename='water_tank_par.png']
8. **HumidifiedRoom Internal Block Diagram**: This diagram is part of the *HumidifiedRoom* block. This internal block diagram depicts the information flow of the vapor going into the room and the humidity that results from this vaporization. The information about the vapor being inputted into the room goes through a unit conversion with the part property *mLpH2mLpS* (typed by block *VolumeConversion*) before passing on the information to part property*rH* (typed by *RelativeHumidity*). This information, along with information about current relative humidity of the room from part property*rH* and information about the saturated vapor pressure from part property*sVP* (typed by *SaturationVaporPressure*), is used to calculate the amount of humidity in the room. The part property*toPercentage* (typed by *PercentageConversion*) converts the signal about room humidity from fractions into a percentage value and sends this new information about the percentage value back to the humidifier system. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. SaturationVaporPressure Parametric Diagram : This parametric diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flows associated with the part property sVP (typed by SaturationVaporPressure ) in the HumidifiedRoom internal block diagram. Numerical calculations in this diagram provide information about the room’s vapor pressure to be sent as a signal used to calculate current room humidity in the HumidifiedRoom internal block diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='saturation_vapor_pressure_par.png']
10. RelativeHumidity Internal Block Diagram : This internal block diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flows associated with the part property rH (typed by RelativeHumidity ) in the HumidifiedRoom internal block diagram. It calculates the current humidity of the room given the room’s vapor pressure levels as well as the incoming vapor coming from the humidifier system. [ATTACHMENT filename='relative_humidity_ibd.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In this model, there are two main components that interacted with each other: the room and the humidifier system. The purpose of the humidifier is to control the humidity of the room once a user has selected a humidity level for the room. State machines are used to show the control of humidity in the room, both to depict the user’s input and the humidifier’s control system. For the sake of brevity, the following SysML v1 diagrams form only the core set of diagrams from the SysML v1 model, and include all three state machines used in this example.</p><ol><li><strong>HumidifierSystem Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is part of the <em>HumidifierSystem </em>block. The flow between the part properties <em>room</em> (typed by <em>HumidifiedRoom</em>) and <em>humidifier</em> (typed by <em>Humidifier</em>) are shown to be going through ports. These ports are the interfaces between the two system components, and contain numeric information about the humidity and vapor going between the two components.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="humidifier_system_ibd.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><strong>Humidifier Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is part of the <em>Humidifier</em> block. The flow within the humidifier system are depicted in this diagram. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">With information about the room’s current humidity from the <em>humidity_in</em> port, and control signal from the humidity control system from the part property control (typed by<em> Control</em>), the part property <em>heaterControl </em>(typed by <em>HeaterControl</em>) uses a state machine to decide whether or not to turn on the heater control system in order to generate vapor in the part property <em>vaporgenerationplant </em>(typed by <em>VaporGenerationPlant</em>). The part property<em> vaporgenerationplant</em> also uses information from the part property control to determine the amount of vapor released into the room (and consumed from the part property<em> watertank</em>, typed by<em> WaterTank</em>) as well as let <em>control</em>  know the temperature of water in the vapor generation system. The part property <em>control</em> also uses information from when the user wants to turn on the humidifier from the part property <em>usage</em> (typed by <em>UsageScenario</em>) and information about the amount of water in the water tank from the part property<em> watertank</em>.</span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="humidifier_ibd.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>UsageScenarioStateMachine State Machine Diagram</strong>: This state machine diagram depicts the behavior of the part property usage in the <em>Humidifier </em>internal block diagram and it determines when (as in, at which times) the humidifier system should humidify the room.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="usage_scenario_stm.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>ControlStateMachine State Machine Diagram</strong>: This state machine diagram depicts the behavior of the part property<em> control  </em>in the <em>Humidifier </em>internal block diagram and it determines the operation of the heater control system <em>heatercontrol  </em>and the vapor generation plant <em>vaporgenerationplant</em> based on information about the water levels in the tank from the <em>watertank</em>, the temperature of vaporization from the <em>vaporgenerationplant</em>, and the operation of when the humidifier system should be started from<em> usage</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="control_stm.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>HeaterControlSM State Machine Diagram</strong>: This state machine diagram depicts the behavior of the part property<em> heaterControl</em> in the <em>Humidifier </em>internal block diagram. It uses information from the room’s current humidity from the <em>humidity_in  </em>pin, the target humidity from the part property<em> targetHumidity</em>, and the control signal from<em> control  </em>in order to decide whether or not to turn on the heater control system for the generation of vapor with <em>vaporgenerationplant</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="197"><ri:attachment ri:filename="heater_control_stm.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>VaporGenerationPlant Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This internal block diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flow associated with the part property <em>vaporgenerationplant</em> in the <em>Humidifier </em>internal block diagram. Given the control signal from <em>control  </em>for its water fan and the control signal from the <em>heaterControl</em>, after the numeric calculations it gives information about the water temperature to the <em>control</em> as well as information about the generated vapor from the<em> watertank</em> and for the room.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="vaporGenerationPlantCalculation_ibd.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>WaterTank Parametric Diagram</strong>: This parametric diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flows associated with the part property <em>watertank</em> in the <em>Humidifier </em>internal block diagram. Given the information about water consumed for vapor generation from <em>vaporgenerationplant</em>, numeric calculations provide information about the volume of remaining water and this new information is sent to the<em> control</em> part property.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="water_tank_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><p><strong>HumidifiedRoom Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is part of the <em>HumidifiedRoom</em> block. This internal block diagram depicts the information flow of the vapor going into the room and the humidity that results from this vaporization. The information about the vapor being inputted into the room goes through a unit conversion with the part property <em>mLpH2mLpS</em> (typed by block <em>VolumeConversion</em>) before passing on the information to part property<em> rH</em> (typed by <em>RelativeHumidity</em>). This information, along with information about current relative humidity of the room from part property<em> rH</em> and information about the saturated vapor pressure from part property<em> sVP</em> (typed by <em>SaturationVaporPressure</em>), is used to calculate the amount of humidity in the room. The part property<em> toPercentage</em> (typed by <em>PercentageConversion</em>) converts the signal about room humidity from fractions into a percentage value and sends this new information about the percentage value back to the humidifier system.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="humidified_room_ibd.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><strong>SaturationVaporPressure Parametric Diagram</strong>: This parametric diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flows associated with the part property <em>sVP</em> (typed by<em> SaturationVaporPressure</em>) in the<em> HumidifiedRoom</em> internal block diagram. Numerical calculations in this diagram provide information about the room’s vapor pressure to be sent as a signal used to calculate current room humidity in the <em>HumidifiedRoom </em>internal block diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="saturation_vapor_pressure_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>RelativeHumidity Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This internal block diagram depicts numerical calculations for the informational flows associated with the part property <em>rH</em> (typed by <em>RelativeHumidity</em>) in the <em>HumidifiedRoom </em>internal block diagram. It calculates the current humidity of the room given the room’s vapor pressure levels as well as the incoming vapor coming from the humidifier system.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="relative_humidity_ibd.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562329 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05225: Description of the ElectricCircuit.mdzip SysML v1 models

- page_id: `249562329`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562329/Description+of+the+ElectricCircuit.mdzip+SysML+v1+models
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:37:14.999+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Electric Circuit sample
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In this model an electric circuit is built using a source, a ground, two resistors, a capacitor, an inductor, as well as ports that allow to connect these circuit components to show the flow (and potential to flow) of charge from one component to the other. In total, there are 8 SysML v1 diagrams in this model:

1. CircuitComponents Block Definition Diagram : This diagram contains a block describing a generic two-pin (ports) electrical component class, TwoPinElectricalComponent , that serves as a generalization of other blocks. The generalization contains a block describing the Resistor class and its properties, a block describing the Inductor class and its properties, a block describing the Capacitor class and its properties, and a block describing the Source class and its properties. There is also a block describing the Ground class that is needed as a reference point for the flow potential (voltage) of charged particles in an electric circuit. [ATTACHMENT filename='circuit_components_bdd.png']
2. CircuitConstraints Block Definition Diagram : Mathematical expressions are provided in constraint blocks ResistorConstraint , CapacitorConstraint , InductorConstraint , and SourceConstraint that extend the mathematical constraints and parameters defined in BinaryElectricalComponentConstraint for each of their respective constraint block classes. These four constraint blocks, along with the mathematical expressions in the GroundConstraint constraint block, describe the flow and potential to flow behavior of charged electric particles that go through different components in the electric circuit. [ATTACHMENT filename='circuit_constraints_bdd.png']
3. Circuit Internal Block Diagram : This diagram is part of the Circuit block, and it shows layout of components in the electric circuit design. The links between the ports of the components depict the physical interactions that occur in the electric domain, that is the flow (current) and potential to flow (voltage) of the charged particles that move through the electric circuit. [ATTACHMENT filename='electric_circuit_ibd.png']
4. Capacitor Parametric Diagram : The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the CapacitorConstraint constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the Capacitor block (from the CircuitComponents block definition diagram). [ATTACHMENT filename='capacitor_par.png']
5. Ground **Parametric**Diagram : The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the GroundConstraint constraint block are bound to the potential to flow property of the Ground block’s port (from the CircuitComponents block definition diagram). [ATTACHMENT filename='ground_par.png']
6. Inductor **Parametric**Diagram : The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the InductorConstraint constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the Inductor block (from the CircuitComponents block definition diagram). [ATTACHMENT filename='inductor_par.png']
7. Resistor **Parametric**Diagram : The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the ResistorConstraint constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the Resistor block (from the CircuitComponents block definition diagram). [ATTACHMENT filename='resistor_par.png']
8. **Source **Parametric**Diagram**: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the *SourceConstraint*constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *Source*block (from the *CircuitComponents* block definition diagram). [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In this model an electric circuit is built using a source, a ground, two resistors, a capacitor, an inductor, as well as ports that allow to connect these circuit components to show the flow (and potential to flow) of charge from one component to the other. In total, there are 8 SysML v1 diagrams in this model:</p><ol><li><strong>CircuitComponents Block Definition Diagram</strong>: This diagram contains a block describing a generic two-pin (ports) electrical component class, <em>TwoPinElectricalComponent</em>, that serves as a generalization of other blocks. The generalization contains a block describing the <em>Resistor</em> class and its properties, a block describing the <em>Inductor</em> class and its properties, a block describing the<em> Capacitor  </em>class and its properties, and a block describing the <em>Source</em> class and its properties. There is also a block describing the <em>Ground</em> class that is needed as a reference point for the flow potential (voltage) of charged particles in an electric circuit.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="circuit_components_bdd.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>CircuitConstraints Block Definition Diagram</strong>: Mathematical expressions are provided in constraint blocks <em>ResistorConstraint</em>, <em>CapacitorConstraint</em>,<em> InductorConstraint</em>, and <em>SourceConstraint</em> that extend the mathematical constraints and parameters defined in <em>BinaryElectricalComponentConstraint  </em>for each of their respective constraint block classes. These four constraint blocks, along with the mathematical expressions in the <em>GroundConstraint  </em>constraint block, describe the flow and potential to flow behavior of charged electric particles that go through different components in the electric circuit.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="circuit_constraints_bdd.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>Circuit Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is part of the <em>Circuit</em> block, and it shows layout of components in the electric circuit design. The links between the ports of the components depict the physical interactions that occur in the electric domain, that is the flow (current) and potential to flow (voltage) of the charged particles that move through the electric circuit.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="electric_circuit_ibd.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Electric Circuit sample" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><strong>Capacitor Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the <em>CapacitorConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>Capacitor</em> block (from the <em>CircuitComponents</em> block definition diagram).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="capacitor_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>Ground <strong>Parametric </strong>Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the <em>GroundConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the potential to flow property of the <em>Ground</em> block’s port (from the<em> CircuitComponents</em> block definition diagram).<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ground_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>Inductor <strong>Parametric </strong>Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the<em> InductorConstraint  </em>constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the<em> Inductor</em> block (from the <em>CircuitComponents  </em>block definition diagram).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="inductor_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>Resistor <strong>Parametric </strong>Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the<em> ResistorConstraint </em>constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>Resistor</em> block (from the <em>CircuitComponents</em> block definition diagram).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resistor_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><p><strong>Source <strong>Parametric </strong>Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the <em>SourceConstraint  </em>constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>Source  </em>block (from the <em>CircuitComponents</em> block definition diagram).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="source_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562354 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05226: Description of the Hudraulics.mdzip SysML v1 models

- page_id: `249562354`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562354/Description+of+the+Hudraulics.mdzip+SysML+v1+models
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:36:35.270+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Hydraulics sample
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In this model two tanks filled with unequal amounts of a fluid are connected at the bottom of each tank by a pipe. The flow of the fluid from the tank with more fluid to the tank with less fluid is modeled in SysML v1, where it can be exported into Modelica or Simulink/Simscape for simulation. There are 5 SysML diagrams in this model:

1. **TwoTanksComponents Block Definition Diagram**: This diagram contains a block describing the *Tank* class and its properties, a block describing the *Pipe*class and its properties, and interface block describing the conserved substance flow rate and potential to flow. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. TwoTankConstraints Block Definition Diagram : Mathematical expressions are provided in constraint blocks PipeConstraint and TankConstraint to show the relationship between the flow and pressure of the conserved substance (the fluid) that goes through Tank and Pipe components. [ATTACHMENT filename='two_tanks_constraints_bdd.png']
3. Tank Parametric Diagram : The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the TankConstraint constraint block are bound to the properties of the Tank block (from the ****TwoTanksComponents block definition diagram). [ATTACHMENT filename='tank_par.png']
4. Pipe Parametric Diagram : The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the PipeConstraint constraint block are bound to the properties of the Pipe block (from the TwoTanksComponents block definition diagram). [ATTACHMENT filename='pipe_par.png']
5. ConnectedTanksIBD Internal Block Diagram : This diagram is a part of the ConnectedTanks block. The interconnections between the blocks and ports are shown in this diagram. There are two part properties ( fluidReservoir1 and fluidReservoir2 ) of type Tank and a part flowMedium of type Pipe as well as ports that determine the interface between the tanks and pipe where the fluid flows through (characterized by flow rate and potential to flow in the connectors between the ports). [ATTACHMENT filename='connected_tanks_ibd.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In this model two tanks filled with unequal amounts of a fluid are connected at the bottom of each tank by a pipe. The flow of the fluid from the tank with more fluid to the tank with less fluid is modeled in SysML v1, where it can be exported into Modelica or Simulink/Simscape for simulation. There are 5 SysML diagrams in this model:</p><ol><li><p><strong>TwoTanksComponents Block Definition Diagram</strong>: This diagram contains a block describing the <em>Tank</em> class and its properties, a block describing the <em>Pipe </em>class and its properties, and interface block describing the conserved substance flow rate and potential to flow. </p><p><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="two_tanks_components_bdd.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><strong>TwoTankConstraints Block Definition Diagram</strong>: Mathematical expressions are provided in constraint blocks<em> PipeConstraint  </em>and <em>TankConstraint  </em>to show the relationship between the flow and pressure of the conserved substance (the fluid) that goes through <em>Tank</em> and <em>Pipe</em> components.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="two_tanks_constraints_bdd.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><strong>Tank Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the <em>TankConstraint  </em>constraint block are bound to the properties of the <em>Tank </em> block (from the<em><strong> </strong>TwoTanksComponents</em> block definition diagram).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="tank_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>Pipe Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the <em>PipeConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties of the <em>Pipe</em> block (from the <em>TwoTanksComponents  </em>block definition diagram).<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="pipe_par.png" /></ac:image></li><li><strong>ConnectedTanksIBD Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is a part of the <em>ConnectedTanks</em> block. The interconnections between the blocks and ports are shown in this diagram. There are two part properties (<em>fluidReservoir1 </em>and <em>fluidReservoir2</em>) of type <em>Tank</em> and a part <em>flowMedium</em> of type<em> Pipe</em> as well as ports that determine the interface between the tanks and pipe where the fluid flows through (characterized by flow rate and potential to flow in the connectors between the ports).<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="connected_tanks_ibd.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562401 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05227: Description of the SignalProcessor.mdzip SysML v1 models

- page_id: `249562401`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562401/Description+of+the+SignalProcessor.mdzip+SysML+v1+models
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:35:34.799+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Signal processor sample
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In this model, there are three main components: an input signal generator, a signal processor, and an output signal scope. The signal flow from the input generator to the output scope is modeled by 10 SysML v1 diagrams.

1. **SignalProcessorComponents Block Definition Diagram**: This diagram contains a block describing a generic two-pin (ports) signal component class, *TwoPinSignalComponent*, that is a generalization of other blocks. The generalization contains a block describing the *Amplifier*class and its properties, a block describing the *HighPassFilter* class and its properties, and a block describing the*LowPassFilter* and its properties. The block describing the *Mixer* class (and its properties) is related to the *RealSignalInElement* and *RealSignalOutElement*interface blocks as well as *TwoPinSignalComponent* block. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. **SignalProcessorConstraints Block Definition Diagram**: Mathematical expressions are provided in constraint blocks *AmplifierConstraint*, *LowPassFilterConstraint*, *HighPassFilterConstraint* that make use of the parameters used in the constraint block *BinarySignalComponentConstraint*.These three constraint blocks, along with the *MixerConstraint* constraint block, describe the filtering and mixing of the generated signal that goes through the signal processor. The *SignalSinkConstraint*constraint block expresses the output of the signal processor as the output of the entire system, and the *SinalSourceConstraint*constraint block provides the generated input signal that flows through the system. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. **TestBed Internal Block Diagram**: This diagram is part of the *TestBed*block. The flow of signal between the part properties *inputSignal* (typed by *SignalSource*), *dSP* (typed by *SignalProcessor*), and *scopeSignalOutput*(typed by *SignalSink*) is shown to be going through ports typed by *RealSignalInElement* and *RealSignalOutElement*interface blocks. These ports are the interface between the three connected system components. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. **SignalSource Parametric Diagram**: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the *SignalSourceConstraint* constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *SignalSource* block (from the *SignalProcessorComponents* block definition diagram). 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. **SignalSink Parametric Diagram**: The constraint of the *SignalSinkConstraint* constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *SignalSink* block (from the *SignalProcessorComponents*block definition diagram). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. **SignalProcessor Internal Block Diagram**: This diagram is part of the *SignalProcessor*block and shows how the signal becomes amplified, filtered, and then remixed again. The flow of signal between the part properties *hpf* (typed by *HighPassFilter*), *lpf* (typed by*LowPassFiler*), *a* (typed by *Amplifier*), and*m* (typed by *Mixer*) is shown to be going through ports typed by *SignalPin*. These ports serve as interfaces where the signal flows from one part to the other. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. **Amplifier Parametric Diagram**: The constraints of the*AmplifierConstraint* constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *Amplifier* block (from the *SignalProcessorComponents*block definition diagram). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. **LowPassFilter Parametric Diagram**: The constraint of the *LowPassFilterConstraint* constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *LowPassFilter* block (from the *SignalProcessorComponents* block definition diagram). 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. **HighPassFilter Parametric Diagram**: The constraint of the *HighPassFilterConstraint*constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *HighPassFilter* block (from the *SignalProcessorComponents* block definition diagram). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. **Mixer Parametric Diagram**: The constraint of the *MixerConstraint* constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the *Mixer*block (from the*SignalProcessorComponents* block definition diagram). [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In this model, there are three main components: an input signal generator, a signal processor, and an output signal scope. The signal flow from the input generator to the output scope is modeled by 10 SysML v1 diagrams.</p><ol><li><p><strong>SignalProcessorComponents Block Definition Diagram</strong>: This diagram contains a block describing a generic two-pin (ports) signal component class, <em>TwoPinSignalComponent</em>, that is a generalization of other blocks. The generalization contains a block describing the <em>Amplifier </em>class and its properties, a block describing the  <em>HighPassFilter</em> class and its properties, and a block describing the<em> LowPassFilter</em> and its properties. The block describing the <em>Mixer</em> class (and its properties) is related to the <em>RealSignalInElement</em> and <em>RealSignalOutElement </em>interface blocks as well as <em>TwoPinSignalComponent</em> block.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="signal_processor_components_bdd.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>SignalProcessorConstraints Block Definition Diagram</strong>: Mathematical expressions are provided in constraint blocks <em>AmplifierConstraint</em>, <em>LowPassFilterConstraint</em>, <em>HighPassFilterConstraint</em> that make use of the parameters used in the constraint block <em>BinarySignalComponentConstraint</em>.These three constraint blocks, along with the <em>MixerConstraint</em> constraint block, describe the filtering and mixing of the generated signal that goes through the signal processor. The <em>SignalSinkConstraint  </em>constraint block expresses the output of the signal processor as the output of the entire system, and the <em>SinalSourceConstraint  </em>constraint block provides the generated input signal that flows through the system.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="signal_processor_constraints_bdd.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>TestBed Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is part of the <em>TestBed </em>block. The flow of signal between the part properties <em>inputSignal</em> (typed by <em>SignalSource</em>), <em>dSP</em> (typed by <em>SignalProcessor</em>), and <em>scopeSignalOutput </em>(typed by  <em>SignalSink</em>) is shown to be going through ports typed by <em>RealSignalInElement</em> and <em>RealSignalOutElement </em>interface blocks. These ports are the interface between the three connected system components.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="testBed_ibd.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>SignalSource Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint parameters (variables in the mathematical expressions of the constraints) of the <em>SignalSourceConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>SignalSource</em> block (from the <em>SignalProcessorComponents</em> block definition diagram).<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="signal_source_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>SignalSink Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint of the <em>SignalSinkConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>SignalSink</em> block (from the <em>SignalProcessorComponents  </em>block definition diagram).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="signal_sink_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>SignalProcessor Internal Block Diagram</strong>: This diagram is part of the <em>SignalProcessor  </em>block and shows how the signal becomes amplified, filtered, and then remixed again. The flow of signal between the part properties  <em>hpf</em> (typed by  <em>HighPassFilter</em>),  <em>lpf</em> (typed by<em> LowPassFiler</em>), <em>a</em> (typed by <em>Amplifier</em>), and<em> m</em> (typed by <em>Mixer</em>) is shown to be going through ports typed by <em>SignalPin</em>. These ports serve as interfaces where the signal flows from one part to the other.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="signal_processor_ibd.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>Amplifier Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraints of the<em> AmplifierConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>Amplifier</em> block (from the <em>SignalProcessorComponents  </em>block definition diagram).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="amplifier_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>LowPassFilter Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint of the <em>LowPassFilterConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>LowPassFilter</em> block (from the <em>SignalProcessorComponents</em> block definition diagram).<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="low_pass_filter_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>HighPassFilter Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint of the <em>HighPassFilterConstraint  </em>constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>HighPassFilter</em> block (from the <em>SignalProcessorComponents</em> block definition diagram).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="high_pass_filter_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li><li><p><strong>Mixer Parametric Diagram</strong>: The constraint of the <em>MixerConstraint</em> constraint block are bound to the properties and ports of the <em>Mixer  </em>block (from the<em> SignalProcessorComponents</em> block definition diagram).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="mixer_par.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243967390 space=MT version=7 -->
## PAGE 05228: Diagram aspects

- page_id: `243967390`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243967390/Diagram+aspects
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-09-10T12:15:07.949+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagramming
- labels: ['diagram-stereotype', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Diagram aspects allow you to create different views, e.g., electrical, mechanical, and optical, for the same system structure. The aspects functionality is driven by stereotypes, which allows you to use predefined aspects (only included in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v1 modeling' space=''] or other customizations) or custom aspects for diagrams. This enables you to:

- Set any number of aspects for one diagram.
- Drill down navigation from the Part shape to its internal structure.
- Create an Aspect diagram directly from the Part shape.
- Display parts and ports according to aspects.
- Use aspects with multiple [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Layout templates' space=''].

The figure below demonstrates the usage of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Finding predefined diagram aspects' space='']predefined diagram aspects. Three SysML Internal Block Diagrams of the same *Climate Control Hardware* system are of different aspects: electrical and communication. The Internal Block Diagram with the electrical aspect shows only the electrical structure of the *Climate Control Hardware* system. Correspondingly, the IBD with the communication aspect shows only the communication structure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The Climate Control Hardware views through different aspects. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

The main workflow to start using the diagrams aspect functionality in your model is the following.

To use diagram aspects

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a new project' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening projects' space=''] one.
2. Enable the diagrams aspect option. Enabling or disabling diagram aspects option>>
3. Make sure that predefined aspects are enough for you. Finding predefined diagram aspects>>
4. If predefined aspects are not what you expect, create your own aspect that you can apply to a diagram. Creating custom aspects>>
5. Create a new aspect diagram: - by creating a new diagram with already applied aspect: Creating an aspect diagram>> 
- by applying predefined (#3) or custom aspects (step #4) for an existing diagram. Applying or removing aspect for preexisting diagrams>>
6. After your model contains diagrams with defined aspects, you can display parts and ports according to aspects and navigate among aspect diagrams.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Layout templates' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Layout templates' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying rake icon' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profile diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generalization' space='MED']

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the **Diagram aspects** sample model. To open it:

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram aspects.mdzip'].
- Find in the modeling tool: *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\diagram aspects.mdzip.*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Generalization" /></ac:link></span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Sample model</strong></p><p>The model used in the figures of this page is the <strong>Diagram aspects</strong> sample model. To open it:</p><ul><li data-uuid="b68a3ca8-d542-4b42-a02b-9e12cfdb0217"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Download <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="diagram aspects.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.</span></li><li data-uuid="8ef1f885-3ff3-4cb6-ba1d-4aea7220c0aa"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span class="confluence-link">Find in the modeling tool: <em><span>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\SysML v1\diagram aspects.mdzip.</span></em></span></span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243967145 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05229: Diagram banners

- page_id: `243967145`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243967145/Diagram+banners
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:22.113+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagramming
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

923016198

923016209

923016197

**On this page**

923016199

You can create diagram banners, such as a header and/or footer, to display important information on the diagram. The banners are not included in the diagram frame but are contained within the diagram pane; thus, if you choose to export the diagram ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving as image' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Printing' space='']), the banners are included. The diagram banners can be created for all diagram types, e.g. tables, matrices, or maps.

##### Creating diagram banners

To create a diagram banner

1. Right-click the diagram. In the shortcut menu, select Stereotype . The Stereotype selection menu opens.
2. In the Stereotype selection menu, select the «banner» stereotype check mark. Click the Apply button. [ATTACHMENT filename='stereotype_selection.png']
3. Right-click the diagram again and open its Specification window.
4. In the Specification window, click the Tags property group.
5. Under the «banner» stereotype, select the text tag and click the Create Value button. [ATTACHMENT filename='banner_creation.png']
6. In the Value box on the right side of the dialog, write the text that you want to have displayed on the diagram banner.
7. In the toolbar of the right-side compartment, next to the Display option, select the position for the diagram banner from the dropdown menu.
  1. Header - the diagram banner will be displayed at the top of the diagram. This is the default option.
  2. Footer - the diagram banner will be displayed at the bottom of the diagram.
  3. Both - the diagram banner will be displayed at the top and bottom of the diagram.
  4. **None** - the diagram banner will not be displayed on the diagram. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. (Optional) To create additional banners for the diagram, click the **Add** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button and follow the steps 6-7.
9. Click **Close**. The diagram banner is created. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Editing diagram banners

To edit the diagram banner text and/or display position

1. Right-click the diagram and open its **Specification window**.
2. In the Specification window, click the Tags property group.
3. Select the **text** value under the «banner» stereotype.
4. In the Value box on the right-side compartment, select the text value you want to edit and click the Edit [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
5. Edit the text of the banner and/or the display position via the Display option for the selected text value.
6. Click Close .

##### Removing diagram banners

To remove a diagram banner

1. Right-click the diagram and open its **Specification window**.
2. In the Specification window, click the Tags property group.
3. Select the **text** value under the «banner» stereotype.
4. Do one of the following:
  1. To remove all banners, click the Remove Value button.
  2. To remove a single banner, in the Value box on the right-side compartment, select the text value you want to remove and click the Remove [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
5. Click Close .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="866d6e0e-d37d-4df7-917d-50c0dab3be82"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">923016198</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0e48e154-0cd8-423d-bb47-25e98da602bc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">923016209</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fa0c7ef0-9a5c-439e-a26f-d5b873059202"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">923016197</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="536f2e85-0cff-438a-a633-cf63ce6a14e5" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cebdb548-feb1-4a97-bd5c-22aa884652c7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">923016199</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can create diagram banners, such as a header and/or footer, to display important information on the diagram. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249564910 space=MT version=2 -->
````
## PAGE 05230: Diagram Creation Wizard

- page_id: `249564910`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249564910/Diagram+Creation+Wizard
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:25.628+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Dialogs
- labels: ['diagram-creation-wizard', 'cv-3-creation-wizard', 'pv-2-creation-wizard', 'acv-2-creation-wizard', 'stv-3-creation-wizard', 'npv-1-creation-wizard', 'ncv-5-creation-wizard', 'lr-lines-of-development-creation-wizard', 'cr-capability-roadmap-creation-wizard', 'unrestored-unknown-attachment', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Diagram Creation Wizard** is used to create the following diagrams:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='CV-3 Capability Phasing' space=''] (DoDAF)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='PV-2 Project Timelines' space=''] (DoDAF)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='MODAF. AcV-2 Programme Timelines' space=''] (MODAF)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='MODAF. StV-3 Capability Phasing' space=''] (MODAF)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='NPV-1 Programme Portfolio Relationships' space=''] (NAF v.3)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='NCV-5 Capability to Organisational Deployment Mapping' space=''] (NAF v.3)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lr Lines of Development' space=''] (NAF v.4)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cr Capability Roadmap' space=''] (NAF v.4)
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Personnel Availability Gantt Chart' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Strategic Phasing diagram' space='']

The name of this wizard depends on particular diagram name: **<diagram name> Creation Wizard**:

- CV-3 Creation Wizard
- PV-2 Creation Wizard
- AcV-2 Creation Wizard
- StV-3 Creation Wizard
- NPV-1 Creation Wizard
- NCV-5 Creation Wizard
- Lr Lines of Development Creation Wizard
- Cr Capability Roadmap Creation Wizard
- Personnel Availability Gantt Chart Creation Wizard
- Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart Creation Wizard
- Strategic Phasing Creation Wizard

To open the **Diagram Creation Wizard**

1. Do one of the following:
  - Press Ctrl+N.
  - From the main menu, select Diagrams > Create Diagram .
  - Right-click the <view name> Package and select Create Diagram > <diagram name> .
  - In the main toolbar, click Create Diagram and in the opened window, search for the needed diagram. The Diagram Creation Wizard opens.

The wizard consists of three steps:

truenone

##### Name and owning package specification

In this step, you can specify the diagram name and select or create the owning package. If you want to create a new owning package, use the **Create Owner** button. Also, you can clone an existing package by clicking the **Clone** button.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Capability selection

In this step, you can select Capabilities from the list.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

| Button | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | Selects a Capability.Alternatively, double-click the Capability. |
|  | Removes a Capability. |
|  | Removes all Capabilities. |

##### The chart property setting

In this step, you can set the display style of the chart.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

If you do not wish to use this wizard next time, clear the **Show the wizard next time, when I create <diagram name>** check box.

To restore the usage of the**Diagram Creation Wizard**

1. Select Options > Environment .
2. In the Environment Options dialog select the UAF options group.
3. In the General properties, set the Show PV-2/AcV-2/NPV-1 Creation Wizard each Time Creating New Chart property value to true .

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Lr Lines of Development. Lr Gantt chart' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Cr diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating CV-3 diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating PV-2 diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243967131 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05231: Diagram frame

- page_id: `243967131`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243967131/Diagram+frame
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:21.211+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagramming
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

568201751

568201754

568201753

All UML diagrams have the content area that is restricted by the diagram frame as it is stated in UML specification. The diagram frame is primarily used in cases where the diagrammed element has graphical border elements (like ports for classes and components, entry/exit points on state machines).

By default the diagram frame is displayed on the new diagram pane. The frame is a rectangle in all diagrams, except state machine and activity. State machine and activity diagram frames have rounded corners.

The frame can be re-sized manually by dragging its corners or borders.

To hide the diagram frame, do one of the following

- Right-click the diagram and on the shortcut menu, cancel the selection of the Show Diagram Frame .
- Open the Diagram Properties dialog and set the Show Diagram Frame property to false.

To change the diagram frame properties, do one of the following

- In the Project Options dialog > Symbol styles > Diagram > Diagram Frame options group, change the desired options.
- In the Diagram Properties dialog > Diagram Frame property group, change the properties.

To hide the icon on the diagram frame

- In the Diagram Properties dialog, change the Show Stereotypes property value to Text . [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram_frame.png']

When a new diagram is created, the diagram stereotype icon is displayed on the diagram frame header by default, though you can change it to the context stereotype icon.

To display the context stereotype icon on the diagram frame

- In the **Diagram Properties** dialog > **Diagram Frame** property group, change the **Use Stereotype** property value to **Context**. The **Use Stereotype** property takes effect only in case the **Show Stereotypes** property value is **Text and Icon** or **Icon**. There is a possibility to show the abbreviation of a diagram type instead of the full diagram type in the diagram frame header.

To display the abbreviated diagram type

- In the Diagram Properties dialog > Diagram Frame property group, select the Show Abbreviated Type check box.

The abbreviated diagram types are listed in the following table.

| Diagram name | Abbreviation |
| --- | --- |
| Use Case | uc |
| Communication | comm |
| Sequence | sd |
| State Machine | stm |
| Activity | sct |
| Composite Structure | cs |

568201750

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Note' space='MED']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
